[Page 1]

DNA Sequence-Dependent Binding of Linker Histone gH1 Regulates Nucleosome Conformations Published as part of The Journal of Physical Chemistry virtual special issue “Xiaoliang Sunney Xie Festschrift”. Hong Zhang, Qin Yuan Huo, and Yi Qin Gao*

Downloaded via ECOLE POLYTECHNIC FED LAUSANNE on March 18, 2026 at 02:22:36 (UTC). See https://pubs.acs.org/sharingguidelines for options on how to legitimately share published articles.

Cite This: J. Phys. Chem. B 2022, 126, 6771−6779

ACCESS

Metrics & More

Read Online

Article Recommendations

* Supporting Information sı

ABSTRACT: Sequence-dependent binding between DNA and proteins in chromatin is an essential part of gene expression. Linker histone H1 is an important protein in the regulation of chromatin compartmentalization and compaction, and its binding with the nucleosome is sensitive to the DNA sequence. Although the interactions of H1 and DNA have been widely investigated, the mechanism of nucleosome conformation changes induced by the DNA-sequence-dependent binding with gH1 (globular H1.0) remains largely unclear at the atomic level. In the present molecular dynamics simulations, both linker and dyad DNAs were mutated to investigate the conformational changes of the nucleosome induced by the sequence-dependent binding of gH1 based on the on-dyad binding mode. Our results indicate that gH1 is insensitive to the DNA sequence of the dyad DNA but presents an apparent preference to linker DNA with an AT-rich sequence. Moreover, this specific binding induces the entry/exit region of a nucleosome to a tight conformation and regulates the accessibility of core histones. Considering that the entry/exit region of the nucleosome is a crucial binding site for many functional proteins related to gene expression, the conformational change at this region could represent an important gene regulation signal.

principally responsible for the specific binding with DNA.12 In addition, the modifications and variations of H1 can highly affect its binding with the nucleosome.13,14 Although the interactions between linker histones and nucleosomes have been studied widely both experimentally15 and in silico,16,17 providing information on binding pathway,18 mode,19−21 and affinity,22,23 the investigation on sequence-specific binding of histone gH1 and the nucleosome is limited. Wade and coworkers investigated DNA-sequence-dependent positioning of H1 in nucleosomes by single-pair FRET study.24 However, the effects of DNA-sequence-dependent binding of gH1 on nucleosome conformations remain elusive, and a number of questions need to be answered. For example, why do the nucleosomes in compartment B with AT-rich sequences present a tight and closed state, and the CG-rich compartment A shows an opposite trend? Which binding site of gH1 is the key region responsible for the DNA sequence selection?

INTRODUCTION The distribution of DNA sequence in genome constitutes one important element affecting the properties of chromatin.1 For example, the genome can be divided into two compartments called A and B compartments, representing the active and inactive regions of chromatins, respectively, and CpG dinucleotide is enriched in compartment A.2,3 At the same time, chromatin segregation is also suggested to be highly related to the sequence features of DNA.4,5 In addition, the distribution of DNA sequence can affect the sequencedependent binding with specific proteins, playing a central role in gene expression and mammalian development.6,7 For instance, transcription factor p53 can recognize its target DNA sequence and mediate the transcription initiation process at the entry/exit region of the nucleosome.8,9 However, the sequence-sensitive proteins are not limited to the transcription factors, some other proteins can also bind DNA in a sequencespecific manner to perform particular functions. Linker histone H1 is a special protein in the histone family, playing a nonnegligible role in regulating the chromatin structure.10,11 It should be noted that different domains of histone H1 are responsible for different functions. The two disordered domains of H1, namely, C-terminal domain (CTD) and N-terminal domain (NTD), are mainly responsible for the binding affinity, and the conservative globular domain (GD) is © 2022 American Chemical Society

Received: June 1, 2022 Revised: August 17, 2022 Published: September 5, 2022

[Page 2]

Figure 1. (A) Initial structure of the simulation system. The structures shown in blue and orange were built using the all-atom model, and the rest of the system was built using the coarse-grained model. (B) Distances between dyad and linker DNAs used to describe the conformational changes of the nucleosome.

Answering these questions is crucial to understand whether and how the gH1 mediates A/B compartment formation with different sequence features. Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations were shown to be useful to uncover the binding mechanism of H1 and the nucleosome as it can provide atomic details of conformational changes.25,26 Collepardo-Guevara and co-workers used MD simulations to find a disorder-to-order transition of nucleosome NTD in the DNA binding process.27 Papoian and coworkers investigated the binding dynamics of CTD and NTD with a nucleosome particle by coarse graining MD simulations.28 Wade and co-workers published a series of theoretical studies14,17 about the interactions of the nucleosome and the linker histone,29,30 including their off- and ondyad binding modes. In this study, we only focused on the ondyad binding mode and investigated the sequence-dependent binding of linker histone gH1 and its effects on the nucleosome conformation. Coarse-grained and all-atom multiscale MD simulations are performed, with an overall simulation time is 62 μs.

while the other part of the nucleosome is treated by a coarsegrained model. WT433 is a coarse-grained water model, which can describe approximately 11 water molecules using four tetrahedrally interconnected beads. Around 15000 WT4 water molecules were added to solvate the nucleosome, and sodium ions were used to neutralize the charges. DNA bsc134 and ff19SB force fields35 were used for the all-atom DNA and protein models, respectively. The SIRAH force field was used for the coarse-grained model.36,37 The parameters described the combination of all-atom and coarse-grained models were provided by the SIRAH force field.36,37 Simulation Protocol. All simulations were performed using Amber 2038 in the present work. A Berendsen barostat39 was used to maintain the pressure at 1 atm, and Langevin dynamics40 was applied to maintain the temperature at 300 K. All covalent chemical bonds including hydrogen atoms were restrained to their experimental lengths by SHAKE algorithms.41 Hydrogen mass repartition42 was applied to increase the time step of the simulation, and the equation of motions were integrated with a time step of 4 fs. van der Waals and short-range electrostatic interactions were truncated by means of a smoothed 12 Å spherical cutoff. The particle mesh Ewald (PME) algorithm43 was applied to evaluate the long-range electrostatic forces. Simulation on each system was relaxed for 50 ns and run for 2 μs, with five and three parallel simulations performed for the mutation of the linker and the dyad DNA, respectively. A total of 62 μs (see Table S1) of simulations have been carried out in the present work. Steered Molecular Dynamics Simulation. To gain information on the dissociation pathway of the gH1 to the nucleosome, four parallel 200 ns steered molecular dynamics (SMD) simulations (SMD-1) were performed. All SMD simulations were carried out using Amber 20. Starting from one structure of the equilibrium MD simulations of the nucleosome with a wild-type sequence, the histone gH1 was pulled from the binding site, using a pulling force constant of 5000 kcal/mol/Å2. The pulling direction is along the vector from the center of the all-atom DNA molecules (Figure 1A, colored in orange) to the center of histone gH1 (Figure 1A, colored blue), and the pulling force was applied to both groups, to avoid drifting. To test the dependence of the dissociation pathway on the selection of pulling site, another two parallel SMD simulations (SMD-2) were carried out, and

METHODS

Molecular Models. The initial structure of the nucleosome was obtained from the protein data bank (PDB ID: 5NL0).31 It should be noted that the sequence, modifications, and variations of gH1 can affect its binding with the nucleosome. In this study, we focus on the changes of DNA and use only the wild-type gH1.0 of 5NL0 as an exemplary system. The linker DNA (ID from ±74 to ±96) was mutated to palindromic sequences CpG, TpA, TpG, and methylated CpG (5methylcytosine, called CpG-5CM in the present study), respectively. Therefore, including the wild-type sequence, we constructed five simulation systems with varied linker DNA sequences. We also mutated the dyad DNA (ID from 0 to ±4) to CpG and TpA sequences to investigate the effects of a varied dyad DNA sequence on the gH1 binding to the nucleosome. The linker DNA sequences are not mutated for the latter two systems. All mutations were performed using W3DNA tools,32 and all sequences used in this study are presented in the Supporting Information. As shown in Figure 1A, the binding domains of DNA and gH1 were built using an all-atom model, including the entire globular domain of H1, DNA with sequence numbers of ±75 to ±83 and 0 to ±4,

[Page 3]

Figure 2. Snapshots of the SMD simulations corresponding to the separation process of gH1 and the nucleosome. Four parallel simulations were carried out.

Carrero and co-workers18 modeled different binding pathways of gH1 and chromatin using systems of reaction-diffusion equations to compare with different FRAP experimental data. Pfohl and co-workers48 investigated the binding between gH1 and DNA by small-angle X-ray microdiffraction in combination with microfluidics. They suggested that the binding includes two processes, namely, an initial unspecific binding of gH1 to DNA and a rearrangement of molecules within the formed assemblies. Considering that the globular domain of H1 is predominantly responsible for the DNA sequence selection, we paid attention to determine the leading binding region of the globular domain. Therefore, in the present study, we used MD simulations to gain information on the dissociation of the gH1 from the nucleosome. By SMD-1 simulations, we investigate the pulling-off of gH1 from the nucleosome, which is expected to provide information on the dissociation pathway. As shown in Figure 1A, the binding sites of gH1 include three subdomains, namely, site 1 (β1/L1 and β3), site 2 (α1 and α3), and site 3 (β2, β3, α2, α3, and L2). The initial structure for the SMD simulation was obtained from an equilibrium simulation of the nucleosome. The trajectory obtained from the SMD simulation is shown in Figure 2. In the simulation

the pulling site is around 50 Å far from the initial one (see Figure S1). Trajectories Analysis. The MD trajectories were analyzed using VMD44 and CURVES+ programs.45,46 H-bonds in the present work were defined by a distance between the acceptor and the donor atoms of hydrogen less than 3 Å and an donorhydrogen-acceptor angle larger than 160°. The contact maps and residence time of gH1 residues on DNA base pairs were determined using a cutoff of 3 Å. The solvent accessible surface area of histone octamer were calculated using a cutoff 4.5 Å, which is the probe radius. As shown in Figure 1B, a series of distances were employed to describe the conformational changes of nucleosomes.

RESULTS AND DISCUSSION Investigation on the Dissociation Pathway of gH1. The binding of gH1 to the nucleosome and chromatin has been studied for decades.20,30,47,48 McNally and co-workers12 investigated the binding mechanism of linker histone by integrated FRAP analysis. Their results indicate that the Cterminal domain initiates the binding event, and the H1 globular domain binds to the binding site at a later stage.

[Page 4]

trajectories, the dyad and plus-arm DNAs separate from gH1 before the minus-arm DNA, and the leaving order of the first two binding sites is basically random in parallel SMD simulations. The minus-arm DNA presents a persistent binding until the entire gH1 leaves the nucleosome. This finding on the region of gH1 which binds the minus-arm linker DNA (site 2) is consistent with its role in the initiation of gH1 and nucleosome binding revealed by McNally and co-workers.12 All four parallel simulations yield the same dissociation pathway of gH1, suggesting the robustness of the observed results. To test the dependence of the dissociation pathway on the selection of pulling site, we performed two additional parallel SMD simulations (SMD-2) with a new pulling site which is around 50 Å away from the initial one (see Figure S1). Similar results were obtained which support the persistent binding between site 2 and DNA. Sequence Selectivity of the Binding Sites. To further identify the binding modes and sequence selectivity of the three binding sites, we carried out a series of equilibrium simulations for systems of different DNA sequence features. First, to explore the sensibility of gH1 binding to the sequence of linker DNA, we changed the sequence of two linker DNA arms to pure TpA, TpG, CpG, and CpG-5CM, respectively. We mutated the linker DNAs synchronously to provide a uniform sequential environment for the binding of gH1. In the present work, H-bonds formed between gH1 and DNA were chosen to evaluate their binding feature. As shown in Figure 3,

sequence, whereas the CpG and CpG-5CM sequences form fewer H-bonds with histone gH1 than the others. Next, we investigate the sequence selectivity of gH1 to the dyad DNA. Considering that TpA and CpG sequences of linker DNA present the largest difference in their binding to gH1, we used them as examples for mutation in the dyad DNA. As seen from the number of H-bonds formed (Figure S2), histone gH1 presents a weaker response to the DNA sequence of the dyad DNA than that to the linker DNA. In fact, Wade and coworkers suggested that the sequence and conformation of the nucleosome can both affect the binding of linker histone H5.17,29 They also observed asymmetric dynamics of the linker DNAs with different sequences, which may be related to the asymmetric DNA flexibility and unwrapping of the nucleosome.50 Ha and co-workers50 suggested that the relative DNA flexibility controls the unwrapping direction, and the nucleosome unwraps from the stiffer side. In agreement with their studies, the asymmetric dynamics of DNAs and the effects of DNA sequence on the binding of gH1 were both observed in the present work. Interestingly, the asymmetric dynamics of the nucleosome is also observed for the symmetric sequence of linker DNAs. To further investigate the linker DNA symmetry and stiffness, we performed three additional parallel simulations in the absence of gH1 for TpA and CpG sequences. As shown in Figure S3, we defined four angles to investigate the linker DNA symmetry, our results indicate that the angle fluctuation of the TpA sequence is higher than that of the CpG sequence, which can be rationalized by the higher rigidity of the latter. In addition, we found that the angle changes for minus and plus arms, namely, angles 1 and 2 and angles 3 and 4, are very similar. Therefore, we suggest that the stiffness of linker DNA arms may affect the binding with gH1, while this effect is basically symmetrical for both linker DNA arms. This observation suggests that the asymmetric dynamics of the nucleosome in the present work arises from the different binding ability of the gH1 at sites 1 and 2 to linker DNA. Papoian and co-workers28 investigated the contact map of histone H1 domains and DNA. However, the residues and DNA nucleotides that contribute to the binding are yet to be determined. To further quantify the interaction between gH1 and DNA at the three binding sites, we analyzed the interaction network and residence time of gH1 residues interacting with DNA base pairs. The interacting residues of gH1 with different DNA sequences are listed in Table S2 and Figure S4. The total residence time of gH1 in the five parallel simulations is shown in Figure 4, and the results for individual simulation trajectories are shown in Figures S5−S9. For binding site 2, the binding of the TpA minus arm has a significantly longer residence than other sequences. As shown in Figure 4 (the red frame), the length of the wild-type DNA sequence in contact with gH1 is longer than the other three but not the TpA sequence. For binding site 1, TpA, wild-type, and CpG-5CM sequences present a similar network of contacts between gH1 and DNA, whereas CpG and TpG sequences partially lose contacts with gH1 (see Figure 4, the blue frame). Therefore, both binding sites 1 and 2 of gH1 exhibit a DNA sequence dependence. However, considering that binding site 2 is the leading region in the binding process, it likely plays a major role in the DNA sequence selectivity. For the dyad DNA, the contact map is largely similar among the different DNA sequences (see Figure S10). In summary, gH1 shows an obvious DNA-sequence selectivity to linker rather than the dyad DNA. For the former, a preferred binding is

Figure 3. Average number of H-bonds formed between gH1/plus arm (site 1), gH1/minus arm (site 2), and gH1/total linker DNA along the five parallel simulations for different sequences.

the average number of H-bonds formed between gH1 and each linker DNA arm was calculated from the five parallel simulations. Interestingly, the minus arm and histone gH1 can form significantly more H-bonds (site 2) than the plus arm (site 1), independent of the DNA sequence used. This phenomenon is in agreement with the crystal structures of the nucleosome bound to histone gH1,31 validating that the binding site 2 not only is the leading region but also is subject to strong binding. In addition, it can be found that the number of H-bonds formed between gH1 and linker DNA with the TpA sequence is considerably higher than those formed with other sequences, for both plus and minus arms, which agrees well with the studies of Zhurkin49 and Wade.24 The TpG sequence shows a binding pattern similar to the nonmutated

Figure 4. Residence times of gH1 residues interacting with DNA base pairs in all parallel simulations for the various DNA sequences, namely, TpA, CpG, CpG-5CM, TpG, and the wild-type. The red and blue frames represent the interaction network of marked changes at binding site 2 and site 1, respectively.

Figure 5. Proportion of the closest base pair on minus linker DNA closed to the dyad DNA along the five parallel simulations for different DNA sequences.

observed for the TpA sequence. It should be noted that the sequence, modifications, and variations of gH1 can affect its binding with the nucleosome. To analyze the potential effects of the variations of linker histone sequences on the contact between gH1 and DNA, we aligned the sequences of Gallus gallus gH5, and Xenopus laevis gH1. As shown in Figure S11, there are 14 different residues between these two kinds of linker histones. According to our simulations, only five of these residues can interact with DNA directly, namely, K27, S43, E62, S66, and K73. Among these five residues, only K27, S66, and K73 can interact with DNA for a long residence time. Therefore, we suspect that the mutations of residues K27, S66, and K73 in variant linker histones can affect their binding with DNA. To uncover the nature of the observed sequence selectivity, the structural differences among the various systems are analyzed. As shown in Figure 1A, the minor-groove binding represents the primary binding mode. Therefore, we examined the change of the width of the minor groove. As shown in

Figures S12 and S13, this parameter indeed correlates with the variations of binding strength among different sequences. Specifically, the minor groove width of the TpA sequence is narrow which allows it to interact tightly with the contact residues. Conversely, the CpG sequence is characterized by a wide and unfavorable minor groove, to anchor the interacting protein residues. Regulation of Nucleosome Conformation by gH1 Based on Sequence Dependence. With the analyses on sequence-dependent binding between gH1 and the nucleosome, we investigated the conformational changes of the nucleosome resulting from the variation in DNA sequences. Given that gH1 is insensitive to the dyad DNA sequence, we focused only on the effects of linker DNA sequences on the conformation of the nucleosome. First, we defined a series of distances (described in Figure 1B) to identify which base pair is adjacent to the dyad DNA and used such structural information to reflect the tightness of the structure at the entry/exit region of the nucleosome. The decrease of the linker

[Page 6]

Figure 6. Proportion of the closest base pair on plus linker DNA closed to the dyad DNA along the five parallel simulations for different DNA sequences.

Figure 7. Changes of solvent accessible surface area of one of H4 and H2B histone copies along the trajectories. The shaded parts describe the standard deviation. Analyses of other core histones and the other copies of H4 and H2B histones are shown in Figure S24.

B.51 Interestingly, the entry/exit region of the nucleosome plays an important role in gene expression, especially by providing binding sites for many transcription factors and functional enzymes,52,53 e.g., p53 and NSD methyltransferases.54 Therefore, the conformational changes of this region could serve as an important gene regulation signal.55−57 To estimate the effects of DNA sequence on the core histones, we calculated the solvent accessible surface area (SASA) of each histone of the octamer. As shown in Figure 7, we found that the SASA of histone H4 and H2B is smaller for the TpA sequence than for the other sequences. Such a difference can be rationalized by the fact that the TpA sequence induces a tight binding state and results in a rigid conformation of the octamer histones. As shown in Figure S24, the differences of SASA among DNA sequences are small for other histones. The sequence of linker DNA thus has a potential to regulate the accessibility and conformational changes of the octamer, which may further affect the contact of octamer histones with enzymes, especially the critical residues on these histones. It should be mentioned that the tails of core and linker histones play an important role in maintaining the conformation of the nucleosome, enhancing the binding affinity between the histones and DNA.58 All simulations in the present work were performed in the absence of tails, which can induce potential effects on the nucleosome conformations. For example, the increase of H3 accessibilities may be induced

DNA ID close to the dyad DNA indicates that the downstream linker DNA is pulled toward the dyad region by the histone gH1, and therefore a tighter nucleosome structure is formed. As depicted in Figure 5 and Figures S14 and S16, it can be found that, for most sequences studied in the present work, base pairs −77 and −76 are located much closer to the dyad DNA than the others. However, the −75 base pair presents an obvious movement toward the dyad DNA in the TpA system, as a result of the interaction between gH1 and the minus arm. In addition, we show changes of distances between the plus arm and the dyad DNA in Figure 6 and Figures S15 and S16. Base pairs 77 and 78 are close to the dyad DNA for sequences TpG, CpG, and CpG-5CM. For TpA and nonmutated sequences, the corresponding base pairs are 76 and 77. To provide more conformational details of linker DNAs, we analyzed the changes of DNA twist, tilt, and roll along the trajectories (see Figure S17−S22 in the Supporting Information). In addition, we provided representative simulation snapshots for the min and max DNA IDs adjacent to the dyad DNA (see Figure S23). These results indicate that histone gH1 and linker DNA of the TpA sequence not only form more Hbonds than other sequences but also form a tighter state at both minus and plus arms in the entry/exit region of the nucleosome. These results are in good agreement with the observation that histone gH1 prefers TpA-rich sequences and enriches the rigid and stable region of chromatin, compartment

[Page 7]

by the absence of histone tails at the entry/exit region of the nucleosome.

Author Contributions

CONCLUSIONS In the present computer simulation study, we investigated the DNA-sequence-dependent binding between gH1 and the nucleosome under the on-dyad binding mode. Our simulation provides hints on the dissociation pathway of the H1 globular domain. Our results indicate that site 2 is the leading region of the H1 globular domain for its binding with the nucleosome, consistent with earlier studies.12 We then paid attention to this region in our interrogation of the DNA sequence dependence of gH1 binding. The simulation results indicate that histone gH1 presents a significant preference to the linker DNA of a TpA sequence, especially on the minus arm of DNA (binding site 2 of gH1). Conversely, the interaction of gH1 and the dyad DNA is insensitive to the change of DNA sequences. Finally, we showed that the interaction of gH1 and linker DNA of TpA-rich sequence induces the formation of a tight gH1DNA binding state at the entry/exit region of the nucleosome, affecting the accessibility of core histones. These phenomena provide a conjecture that the DNA-sequence-dependent binding of gH1 contributes to the regulation on the conformational changes of the nucleosome, which are expected to further affect the binding of functional proteins at the entry/ exit region of the nucleosome. Given that a number of protein residues in this region, such as H3K36, possess important biological functions, one would speculate these DNA-dependent effects to play an important role in gene regulation.

H.Z., Q.Y.H., and Y.Q.G. designed research. H.Z. and Q.Y.H. ran the simulations and analyzed the trajectories. H.Z. and Y.Q.G. wrote the paper. Notes
